content:
Reduce latency by running code closer to your users instead of routing every request to a single server. Learn when Edge Functions make sense in Next.js and how to use them without overcomplicating your architecture.

3 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Using Edge Functions in Next.js for Faster Global Response TimesPerformance is one of those things users rarely notice until it becomes a problem. A page that takes an extra second to load might not seem like a big deal during development, but users on the other side of the world can definitely feel the difference.
That’s where Edge Functions come in.
Instead of processing every request from a central server, Edge Functions run closer to your users. The result is lower latency, faster responses, and a smoother experience for globally distributed applications.
In this article, we’ll explore what Edge Functions are, when you should use them, and how they fit into a modern Next.js application.
What Are Edge Functions?
By default, your server code runs in a single region or data center. If a user is thousands of miles away, every request has to travel to that server before getting a response.
